speaker: interviewer
text: November 10, 1996. Survivor, Erna Roth Anolik. Interviewer, Susie Eisenstock. Cherry Hill, New Jersey, United States of America. The language is in English. My name is Susie Paul Eisenstock. The date today is Sunday, November 10, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Erna Roth Anolik. The interview is being conducted in Cherry Hill, New Jersey, the United States of America. And the interview is being conducted in English. What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Erna Roth Anolik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-R-N-A, Erna. Roth, R-O-T-H. And Anolik is A-N-O-L-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. Well, actually Erna is short for Ernestine. So everybody calls me Erna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 15, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 73. 72, actually. Want to be right. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Be 73 soon. What is the city and country of your birth? The city is Uchorod, Czechoslovakia. Would you spell Uchorod, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: U-C-H-O-R-O-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Tell me, what is your father's name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father's name was Ignac Roth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-G-N-A-C, Ignac. Roth, R-O-T-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your father born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Austro-Hungarian Empire, but I cannot tell you what town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- how many years did he complete?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess equivalent to high school. That was as high as he went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a wine merchant. But as a young man, he worked in a bank. And then he went into business for himself, and he was a wine merchant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father ever in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Never talked about it, so I don't know. Was he ever a member of any political party?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a member of any organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What religion was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sect of Judaism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your mother, what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maria Lebovitz Roth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-I-A. Lebovitz is L-E-B-O-V-I-T-- T-Z or sometimes it's spelled with C. I'm not-- and Roth, R-O-T-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was your mother born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in Szeredne in the Austro-Hungarian Empire also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you mind spelling the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-E-R-E-D-N-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that a large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it near something that--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a near Uchorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your mom go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I, I can't really tell you how far she has gone to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother ever have a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she a member of any political party or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had-- my oldest brother was Joseph Roth. Then I had a sister, Olga, another sister, Elizabeth, and a younger brother Erwin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's start with Joseph. Where was Joseph born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in Uchorod on January 7th, 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your brother going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how far did he complete?

speaker: interviewee
text: He finished high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he go on to what we think of as college or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he did not. No, he did not. He went to work--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --after high school. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worked, worked as a salesman in a department store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to remember the name of that department store?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was called Gerstli, G-E-R-S-T-L-I. I think that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Does the department store similar to the way we have department stores today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was not quite as large. It was probably on the smaller scale, but was a department store, nevertheless, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he married while you were still at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is he a survivor of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he left home in 1938 to go to Israel. So he survived in Israel. In fact, he just died one month ago today.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. We'll go on with your sister, Olga.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When and where was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born October 16th, 1919 in Uchorod. And she also finished high school, and then she went on to learn a-- sewing, to be a seamstress.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she marry while you were still at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she did. She married in 1941 in Uchorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll go on with Elizabeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elizabeth was born August 7th or 9th-- and-- I think it was-- must have been 9th, in-- in 1921 in Uchorod. And she finished high school as well. And as my older sister, she, after high school, she went to learn sewing, you know, to be a seamstress.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she marry while you were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned the fourth brother was Erwin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was born May 26, 1932 in Uchorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. To backtrack a little, was Olga a survivor of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, she was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elizabeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was with me all the time, through the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Erwin?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your home, when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. Well, Uchorod was the capital of the Carpathian part of Czechoslovakia, which is the eastern most part of Czechoslovakia. And it was-- I, I think I had a wonderful childhood. We had a lot of friends. A lot of our relatives lived in town, also my father's family mainly. My father had one sister who lived in the same town-- same street as we did. And then we had-- he had two younger brothers who lived in town. My aunt, who lived in the same street as we did, had children very close to our-- my, my age and my sisters ages, brothers ages. So we saw them very often. And I was very friendly with my cousin, who now lives in Pittsburgh. And even in school, I remember a very happy childhood. Had a lot of friends, and parents were quite religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember, for instance, having to always be home on Shabbat for our dinner meal. We have to be there at home. My father came home from the synagogue, and we all had dinner together. And what else shall I tell you about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to the gymnasium and-- in fact, in 1939 or '40, the Hungarians occupied that part of Czechoslovakia. And then I had to transfer from the Czech gymnasium to the Hebrew gymnasium. And so I developed a whole new set of friends, again, in a new school. And it was very pleasant, very nice. But then-- and we were just graduating from the Gymnasium in April 24th, 1944 when there was a, a proclamation. All Jews have to leave home the very next day. So we could not finish our graduation, and we left home the very next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to take you back a little bit because--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to clarify--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: --A few things. Do you remember your address? Your first address of your first home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was Eight Bozos Utca. Bozos is spelled B-O-Z-O-S. Utca means street, and utca is spelled U-T-C-A. And that was in Uchorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the type of home you lived in? Was it a single home or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was an apartment and there were two apartments in the house. And then there was a courtyard. And behind, there were, again, the landlord lived behind, also in an apartment. And we lived there until I was 15 years old. And then in 1938, we moved to another apartment temporarily while my parents had a house built, which we moved into about 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town, what was the percentage of population that were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, since all my friends were Jewish-- until fairly recently, when somebody asked me, I said, at least half the town had to be Jewish, and then I found out it wasn't so. It just seemed to me that way because all my friends and everybody I associated with were Jewish. But I think about 30% of the town must have been Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism before 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: None. None at all. Never. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friends-- let's go back. You said your cousins were your friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your cousins with their names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My closest friend-- cousin was Piri, Piri Weinberger. We went to school together and we used to play around after school, hang around together. And she now lives in Pittsburgh, and we still are very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember my-- the friends from later years, from the Hebrew gymnasium. And a couple of them survived, and I'm still very close to them. My very good friend is Susan White, who now lives in Englewood, New Jersey-- Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey. Then there's Edith Ellis, and she lives in West Orange, New Jersey. So I'm very close to them. Then there's another friend in California, who I see occasionally. But that's, that's about it. Yeah. And there's other ones that survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went to school-- I'm going to take you back to when you were in school prior to 1939-- what were your favorite subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: I always liked math. I always liked math, and I also liked geography. I, generally-- I enjoyed school a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you want to be when you grew up when you were little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that-- that I thought of much later on, when I was in a gymnasium, and I wanted to be a textile engineer. And it so happened that's what I did when I came to the States, became a textile engineer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any remembrances of holidays? You told us about Shabbat, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --tell us about holidays when you were growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Generally, when I think of, of home, of my parents' house, and-- [COUGHS] Excuse me. Those are times, I remember, that we always-- the Shabbat-- being always together and being having dinners always together. And the holidays were very significant. My parents were orthodox, so we always observed every holiday. My father went to synagogue every day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and I remember also having friends in the-- in the building that we lived in. The landlord had a-- a daughter exactly my age. She went to school with me also. They were Hasidic. And I remember, I could go-- sometime I would go to their house on Shabbat or on holidays. And when I went there, you could only speak Yiddish, if you went to their house on holidays, especially when her father was home. You have to, had to speak Yiddish. And I didn't speak Yiddish too well, so I would go there but not very often on holidays or on Shabbat. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: As I said, we observed all the holidays. We observed strictly, and I-- I would go-- what I remember about holidays, for instance, that on Tish'a B'Av, we used to go to my very religious friends. And we used to sit there on the floor and pray and-- certain things come back, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We certainly-- because the families were so large-- like, for instance, Passover, over here, the whole family gets together and a lot of people. We didn't do that, I guess because we lived in apartments and the apartments were not large enough to accommodate very large families.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for, for Passover, we just had immediate family together. And then after the dinner, we would go visiting the relatives or they would come to our house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a lot of my father's family, and as well as my mother's family, they lived in, in small towns. So when they came into the city to visit, they would always stay with us. And that was very pleasant. Nice memories for that. And on the other hand, on-- in the summers when we were on school vacations, I used to go either to my grandparents-- my father's parents-- for a vacation, or sometime to some of my aunts in Szeredne. But I especially remember enjoying very much going to visit my grandparents, because they had a farm and they grew their own vegetables, and fruits, and seeing them milk cows. So it was exciting for me. I used to enjoy going there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else can I tell you? Do you remember your name of your father's parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandfather's name was Herman Roth, and my grandmother's name was, I think it was, Rebecca Roth. And my father's family was a very large family. He had a-- he had three brothers, and must have been at least six or eight sisters. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned some of those aunts and uncles lived in the town. Do you remember what their names were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The aunt who lived in town was Ida. We called her Idushka. And then there was a brother, David, another brother [? Bela-- ?] which is William. And those are the ones who lived in town. Then there was one aunt on my mother's side that I was very close to because my mother came from a very large family. I didn't even know all the aunts and uncles. But I think that most of them were half sisters and brothers to my mother. There was one real sister, and we were very close to her. Her name was Dora Birnbaum. And she lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. And she also had daughters very close to my age. In fact, her-- she had two daughters. The younger one has the same name as I have, and she's very-- I'm even between the two girls so we were very close also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for-- to play when you were little? Did you have toys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many toys. [LAUGHS] We just had to play tag or whatever. But we were mostly playing outside in the yard or whatever. In the winter, we went sleigh riding. We-- in the summer, we were at the river swimming all day long, all afternoon definitely. We spent a lot of time at on at the river. But there weren't too many toys. [LAUGHS] Nobody had too many toys. But I do remember when somebody would come and visit us with a bicycle. I was very anxious to learn to ride a bicycle. So my father would ask whoever's bicycle it was if he could teach me to ride a bicycle. And I learned to ride a bicycle. I was very happy about that. And as a result now, my whole family's bicycle riders. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- did you have any formal religious training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Czechoslovakia, religion was one of the subjects you learned in school. It was-- we went to school from 8:00 to 1:00, and then any-- like gym and religion was taught after the one o'clock class. We'd go back for those classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So religion was taught in school. And it was not taught-- in the-- the Jewish kids went to the Jewish religion class. Catholics would probably go to the Catholic religion, or Protestants to the Protestants. So we were not, the whole group, learning religion together. So we had religion there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, in my family, as, as early as we could learn, we had to learn prayer in the morning and prayer at night. And-- and then later on, I went to the Hebrew gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, I had religion again. There was much more intense. I learned Torah and [YIDDISH]. And-- so I definitely had to learn religion. Yeah. You mentioned your synagogue. Do you remember the name of the synagogue? Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think it was just named after the street that it was on. And it was the Radvanc. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you try to spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-A-D-V-A-N-C. Radvanc.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the rabbi's name of the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned also a river that you played in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of the river?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the river was Uz. U-Z. That's why the name was called Uchorod, because it was named after the river. And also there was a, a castle just above the river and the name horod mean-- I think it's in Russian-- or in Czech-- meant castle. So castle on the Uz, on the river Uz. And that's where the name of the town came from. Uchorod.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also mentioned-- and I'd like to clarify-- that you went and while you were playing with your friends and things like that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your parents doing again?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a wine merchant, and mother, my mother was just homemaker. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she-- was there anybody else in the house with your mother and with you growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a maid who was-- who lived with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were her jobs to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she had the house cleaning and laundry. That was mainly it because my mother did all the cooking. But the-- the maid did the house cleaning and the laundry, ironing, that, that type of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your house-- the original house where you grew up before you got your new home--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. The Apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Where was that located in relation to other things in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the street that the main market was on. The, the market-- we didn't have big supermarkets like we have here. So, for instance, produce and chickens, geese, ducks, and all these things, you bought-- the poultry, you bought it live at the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people from surrounding towns-- farmers-- used to bring their produce and livestock into town every day, but especially on Thursdays, I think. So the market was set up right in our street, on Bozos Utca. The produce, farmers were mainly Bulgarians. And-- so they-- they would bring in the produce and fruits and, and vegetables. So we would buy it-- go to the market and buy it all the time. And that was set up right in front of our house. They would be out there in the morning. By twelve or one o'clock in the afternoon, it was all cleaned up. You wouldn't know there was a market there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but-- and also, the street we lived on was a continuation of Masaryk [? Tere. ?] Masaryk was the president of Czechoslovakia and the main-- main square in town was named after him. It was like a plaza. [? Tere ?] means plaza. So the street I lived on was sort of a continuation of the Masaryk [? Tere, ?] of the main plaza. There was a park there with a statue of the president, Masaryk. And our-- my elementary school was right around the, the same plaza that I went to. And next door to us was a nunnery. That was one of our preoccupations was [INAUDIBLE] to always go spy on the nuns.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] How did your father sell his wine?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a wine cellar, and he sold mainly wholesale. But a lot of Jewish-- on Fridays, a lot of Jewish people came to buy wine from him just for Fridays, because it had to be bought from a Jewish person. Once a non-Jewish person touched a wine, it was no longer kosher. So a lot of Jewish people came on Fridays. But mainly, he sold wholesale to inns and-- I guess mainly for inns. Yeah. And he would-- he would have-- he had a wine cellar. The wine was kept in huge barrels. And I remember in, in the summer, or rather, in the fall, they would harvest the grape, and they would-- he would-- he would make the wine out of it. And I very fondly remember having the new wine before it fermented. And we'd go roast potatoes in the skin and have the-- this new wine with it, which was delicious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you speak any other languages, other than the native language of Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We spoke Hungarian, because my father mainly spoken Hungarian. So in the house, actually, at home we spoke mainly Hungarian. Czech was spoken in school and, and Hungarian at home. And if my parents didn't want us to understand what they said, then they spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Thank you.
